name: icon-list
title: Icon List
description: A native semantic <ul> whose items lead with an Icon instead of a bullet — for benefit lists, feature checklists, and "what you'll get" summaries. Keeps role="list" so it is still announced as a list after list-style is removed (the Safari/VoiceOver gotcha). Leading icons are decorative by default (aria-hidden) or meaningful (role=img with an accessible name) per item; the icon pins to the first text line while multi-line content wraps under the text, not the icon. A default list glyph with per-item overrides, logical properties for RTL, and currentColor stroke for forced-colors safety.
useWhen: A benefits, features, or "what you'll need" list where a glyph (a check bullet) reads better than a plain marker
Marking individual rows differently — e.g. an x "not included" row among check rows
A leading icon must convey status the text does not, needing an accessible name
avoidWhen: A plain ordered or unordered bullet list (use List)
A list of interactive choices the user selects from (use RadioGroup, ComboBox, or DropdownMenu)
Media-rich cards or article summaries (use Collection or CardGroup)

keyboard: Static content — IconList and IconListItem never take focus and add no keyboard behavior
Any interactive element placed inside an item (a Link, a Button) keeps its own native keyboard contract
notes: The <ul> uses list-style: none (the marker is replaced by an icon) but adds an explicit role="list" — Safari/VoiceOver silently drops list semantics from a marker-less <ul> otherwise (the same fix packages/core/src/reset.css applies to raw ul[role="list"], mirrored by List's unstyled variant and Collection).

Leading icons are DECORATIVE by default: with no iconLabel the Icon is aria-hidden and skipped, so a screen reader is not prefixed with 'image' before every line whose meaning the text already carries.
Passing iconLabel on an item flips its icon to MEANINGFUL (role=img with that accessible name), for a status glyph that conveys something the text does not.
Icons stroke with currentColor (never a hardcoded fill), so they inherit text color and stay visible in Windows High Contrast / forced-colors mode; no state is conveyed by color alone.

All spacing uses logical properties (gap, paddingInlineStart:0), so the leading icon sits at the inline-start and the layout mirrors automatically under dir="rtl" with no AmbientDirection needed — these are native elements, not a Base UI primitive.
The item is top-aligned (items-start) with the icon centered on a line-height-tall box, so the icon pins to the first text line while multi-line content wraps under the text, not the icon.
